title: Chocolate and courgette cake
subtitle: A moist chocolate traybake with grated courgette in the batter
excerpt: Grated courgette disappears into this chocolate cake, leaving no trace except moisture. The batter comes together in one bowl without a mixer, and the result is a dense, fudgy traybake that keeps well for three days. A good way to use a glut of summer courgettes.
sourceNotes: Adding grated vegetables to chocolate cake is a technique that became widespread in British home baking in the 2000s, borrowing from the American tradition of carrot cake and zucchini bread. The one-bowl method using oil rather than creamed butter is consistent with American quick-cake techniques. This recipe is synthesised from the established British vegetable-cake tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut into pieces and freeze in a sealed container for up to 2 months. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well for 3 days at room temperature, covered. The moisture from the courgette prevents it drying out.

glossaryTerms.definition: Oil-based cakes use vegetable or sunflower oil instead of creamed butter, which means there is no creaming stage and everything goes into one bowl. The result is a denser, moister crumb that keeps better than a butter cake because oil does not solidify at room temperature the way butter does.

content.text: . There is no creaming, no mixer required and nothing to melt. Grate the courgette on the coarse side of a box grater and add it straight in without squeezing. The water it carries is part of the recipe.
Baked in a 20 x 20 cm tin, the cake comes out about 4 cm tall. If you want frosting, a simple chocolate ganache or cream cheese frosting spread over the cooled cake works well.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C. Grease a 20 x 20 cm square tin and line with baking paper. In a large bowl, whisk together the 150 ml vegetable oil, 200 g caster sugar, 3 eggs and 1 tsp vanilla extract until combined. Stir in the 250 g grated courgette. Sift over the 250 g flour, 50 g cocoa, 1.5 tsp baking powder, half a teaspoon bicarbonate of soda and half a teaspoon salt and fold until just combined. Fold in the 80 g chopped dark chocolate if using.

Scrape the batter into the tin and level the top. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with a few moist crumbs and the surface feels set. The cake will look very dark; check with a skewer rather than relying on colour. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then lift out onto a wire rack. Cut into 16 squares when cool.

items.problem: Cake is very wet in the middle even after 35 minutes
items.solution: The courgette released a lot of water. Large courgettes (over 20 cm) often hold more moisture. Next time squeeze the grated courgette lightly in a clean cloth before adding. Give the cake 5 more minutes and test again.

The cocoa quantity is high. Use a good-quality Dutch-processed cocoa, which is less acidic than natural cocoa. Natural cocoa has a stronger, sharper flavour that can read as bitter in large quantities.
